coredata.dc:title: Achieving efficient incorporation of electron-withdrawing sites into carbon nitride nanosheets for boosting hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Li
Guo, Huazhang
Wang, Zeming
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: There is an acute demand for the development of high-performance photocatalysts for large-scale green H2 production using solar energy. To address this challenge, a novel approach has been proposed for the fabrication of high-performance 0D/2D carbon quantum dot/C3N4 nanosheet heterojunction (CQD/CNN) photocatalytic materials using a tandem synthesis strategy of carboxylated CQD-induced hydrothermal-pyrolysis. The incorporation of CQD into the synthesis of CNN significantly reduces its thickness, making the CQD/CNN low to 2.5 nm, which is a noteworthy one thirty-sixth in height of bulk CNN. The carboxylated CQD play a crucial role in regulating the proportion of oxygen-containing surface functional groups of CQD/CNN. The CQD/CNN heterojunction shows exceptional H2 production rates and demonstrated the ability to convert O2 into H2O2. The H2 production rate of CQD/CNN is gained to be 54.50 mmol g−1 under continuous 3.5 h visible light irradiation, which is a remarkable seven-fold enhancement in performance over bulk CNN. Moreover, the CQD/CNN photocatalyst achieves a maximum H2O2 concentration of 0.763 mmol/L, which is sixteen times higher than that of pure CNN (0.0475 mmol/L). Through elucidating the relationships between morphological/structural modulation and photocatalytic capability, the CO/C–O ratio is found to have the greatest impact on performance, indicating that the electron-withdrawing sites of CQD/CNN can be augmented by incorporating carboxylated CQD, and the electron-withdrawing group can enhance electron conduction and make electron transfer easier to occur, thereby improving the performance of the heterojunctions. Our findings provide a comprehensive framework for achieving desirable photocatalytic properties of C3N4 materials through electron-withdrawing functional group regulation.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The 1g of melamine was dissolved in a mixture of 1.5 μL of the carboxylated CQD and 50 mL of deionized water, and stirred to make the mixture uniform. The homogenized solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor, and heated in the oven at 195 °C for 12 h, After the reactor cool down to room temperature, centrifuged for 3 times with deionized water, and the white solids obtained were dried. Then the product was calcined for 4 h at 580 °C and 5 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The final product was the light-yellow CQD/CNN heterojunction materials, which were ground into powder for use. For comparison, CQD was replaced with CQD-x to produce CNN-x. The screening of experimental conditions (precursor melamine and CQD addition, calcination temperature, and calcination time) is shown in Supporting Figs. S1–2.CO2 and NH3 temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of CQD/CNN and CNN was also investigated to gain deeper insight into the Lewis acid and base active sites. The desorption curves were analyzed in detail, and the results were presented in Fig. 4f. The TPD experiments revealed that the physisorption of CO2 on CNN was comparatively stronger than on CQD/CNN at around 400 °C, confirming the presence of strong Lewis basic sites of CNN. Conversely, the strong peak around 350 °C of CQD/CNN in NH3-TPD demonstrated the chemisorption of NH3, implying the existence of strong acid sites of CQD/CNN attributed to the introduced carboxyl functional groups. Further, the O2-TPD data revealed that CQD/CNN exhibited a higher adsorption capacity of O2 than CNN, which supports the critical role of O2 adsorption in the overall process [ 66–68]. It can be inferred that the preferred adsorption of O2 of CQD/CNN can promote their two-electron reduction of O2 to H2O2, thereby showcasing their potential as an efficient catalyst. Overall, the TPD experiments offer valuable insights into the active sites of the materials and pave the way for the design and optimization of catalysts for various applications.The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21901154), Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022).
The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21901154 ), Shanghai Pujiang Program ( 21PJD022 ).

(b) The stability measurement of CQD/CNN sample under visible light illumination. (c) Photocatalytic H2O2 production by CNN and CQD/CNN. (d) The calculated electron numbers and the corresponding H2O2 selectivity of CNN and CQD/CNN. (e) The H2O2 production activity of CQD/CNN under different sacrificial agents. head.ce:title: Achieving efficient incorporation of electron-withdrawing sites into carbon nitride nanosheets for boosting hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, BaoShan District, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, BaoShan District, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an acute demand for the development of high-performance photocatalysts for large-scale green H2 production using solar energy. To address this challenge, a novel approach has been proposed for the fabrication of high-performance 0D/2D carbon quantum dot/C3N4 nanosheet heterojunction (CQD/CNN) photocatalytic materials using a tandem synthesis strategy of carboxylated CQD-induced hydrothermal-pyrolysis. The incorporation of CQD into the synthesis of CNN significantly reduces its thickness, making the CQD/CNN low to 2.5 nm, which is a noteworthy one thirty-sixth in height of bulk CNN. The carboxylated CQD play a crucial role in regulating the proportion of oxygen-containing surface functional groups of CQD/CNN. The CQD/CNN heterojunction shows exceptional H2 production rates and demonstrated the ability to convert O2 into H2O2. The H2 production rate of CQD/CNN is gained to be 54.50 mmol g−1 under continuous 3.5 h visible light irradiation, which is a remarkable seven-fold enhancement in performance over bulk CNN. Moreover, the CQD/CNN photocatalyst achieves a maximum H2O2 concentration of 0.763 mmol/L, which is sixteen times higher than that of pure CNN (0.0475 mmol/L). Through elucidating the relationships between morphological/structural modulation and photocatalytic capability, the CO/C–O ratio is found to have the greatest impact on performance, indicating that the electron-withdrawing sites of CQD/CNN can be augmented by incorporating carboxylated CQD, and the electron-withdrawing group can enhance electron conduction and make electron transfer easier to occur, thereby improving the performance of the heterojunctions. Our findings provide a comprehensive framework for achieving desirable photocatalytic properties of C3N4 materials through electron-withdrawing functional group regulation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the depletion of fossil energy reserves and the exacerbation of environmental pollution, global efforts have been intensified towards exploring clean and sustainable energy sources. Hydrogen (H2) has been recognized as a promising clean energy vector, owing to its high energy density, sustainability, lack of secondary pollution, and abundant reserves [1–3]. In recent years, the concept of green H2 has gained significant attention, which refers to the production of H2 using clean and pollution-free primary renewable energy sources such as solar energy. The photocatalytic technology, which utilizes semiconductor photocatalysts to promote the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, presents a potential avenue for driving the production of green H2 using solar energy [4]. The performance of photocatalysts plays a pivotal role in the efficiency and scalability of this process, highlighting the urgent need for developing high-performance photocatalysts for large-scale green H2 production [5].

The conversion of solar energy to green H2 energy can address the carbon-neutral challenge through the choosing of suitable photocatalysts [6]. Among the numerous photocatalysts, carbon nitride (C3N4) as a promising semiconductor photocatalyst has garnered considerable attention due to its non-toxicity, structural stability, and foreseeable H2 generation capacity. However, the traditional bulk C3N4 suffers from low photocatalytic efficiency because of severe charge recombination, inadequate visible-light utilization and confined active sites [7–9]. To overcome these limitations, diverse strategies have been established, including morphology modifications, doping, functionalization, and heterojunction construction. Regarding structural regulation, previous studies have confirmed that the use of 2D nanosheets (NSs) or 1D nanotubes can alleviate the dilemma by increasing carrier concentration. Beyond the morphology modulation, the formed composite materials incorporating g-C3N4 with a wide-spectral response semiconductor can further improve its H2 generation behavior by reason of enhanced visible-light absorption [10–12]. Thereby, it is crucial to optimize the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 by exploring a strategy that simultaneously achieves accelerated electron transfer and enhanced visible-light absorption.

Carbon quantum dots (CQD), a relatively new class of carbon-based nanomaterial, have gained significant interest in various applications due to their facile functionalization [13–15]. Although recent studies have reported the successful fabrication of CQD/g-C3N4 composites for photocatalysis applications, unfortunately, these achievements have not clarified the underlying mechanisms of CQD for controlling the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. Notedly, previous investigations have verified that CQD can effectively induce the formation of metal-organic framework (MOF) NSs and boosted the electrocatalytic performance by modulating the functional groups on their surface [15]. Moreover, owing to their large specific surface energy and small size, CQD have been expected to restrain agglomeration and promote the formation of NSs. Therefore, the incorporation of CQD represents a promising strategy for realizing enhanced carrier migration and visible-light utilization in g-C3N4 photocatalysts [16–19].

In the present study, we devised a novel approach to fabricate high-performance photocatalytic materials by designing and synthesizing electron-withdrawing carboxylated CQD through surface charge state modulation [15]. Subsequently, we developed 0D/2D CQD/C3N4 NS heterojunctions (CQD/CNN) using a tandem synthesis strategy of CQD-induced hydrothermal-pyrolysis. The CQD/CNN heterojunctions showed exceptional H2 production rates and demonstrated the ability to convert O2 into H2O2. Through elucidating the relationships between morphological/structural modulation and photocatalytic capability, our findings provide an extensive framework for achieving desirable photocatalytic properties of C3N4 materials through electron-withdrawing functional group regulation.

Melamine (99%), H2PtCl6·6H2O (99.9%), TEOA (99%), Ethanol (99.5%), 1,5-Diaminonaphthalene (98%), Isophthalic Acid (99%), Terephthalic Acid (99%), 1,2,3-Benzenetricarboxylic Acid Hydrate (99%), 1,2,4-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (99%), Trimesic Acid (99%) were obtained from Adamas (Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd., China) and used without any further purification.

0.1g 1,5-diaminonaphthalene and Trimesic Acid were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol solution and ultrasonic dispersion completely. Then the homogenized solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and placed in an oven for heating reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reactor was cool to room temperature, the reacted solution was extracted from a 10 mL syringe and filtered through an organic filter head of 0.22 μm. There obtained the carboxylated CQD. Other CQD use a similar method replacing the acid reagents using isophthalic acid, terephthalic acid, 1,2,3-trimellitic acid and 1,2,4-trimellitic acid, respectively. The obtained CQDs are represented as CQD-1, CQD-2, CQD-3 and CQD-4. i.e. CQD-x.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of preparation of CQD/CNN heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: The 1g of melamine was dissolved in a mixture of 1.5 μL of the carboxylated CQD and 50 mL of deionized water, and stirred to make the mixture uniform. The homogenized solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor, and heated in the oven at 195 °C for 12 h, After the reactor cool down to room temperature, centrifuged for 3 times with deionized water, and the white solids obtained were dried. Then the product was calcined for 4 h at 580 °C and 5 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The final product was the light-yellow CQD/CNN heterojunction materials, which were ground into powder for use. For comparison, CQD was replaced with CQD-x to produce CNN-x. The screening of experimental conditions (precursor melamine and CQD addition, calcination temperature, and calcination time) is shown in Supporting Figs. S1–2.

To prepare the CNN material, 1g of melamine was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and thoroughly stirred. The homogenized solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor. Subsequently, the reactor was heated in an oven at a temperature of 195 °C for a duration of 12 h. Afterward, the reactor was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The resulting solution was subjected to centrifugation with deionized water for three cycles to remove impurities. The resulting white solid was carefully collected and dried. Subsequently, the dried product was calcined in a N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, reaching a final temperature of 580 °C. The calcination process was carried out for a period of 4 h. The resulting material obtained from this process exhibited a light-yellow color and was subsequently ground into a fine powder for future usage.

Hydrogen production test: The 100 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL solvent (DI water: TEOA = 9:1, 210 μL H2PtCl6·6H2O, 4.759 mg/mL), and a closed quartz container with a volume of 100 mL was prepared in the photocatalytic reactor. Inert gas N2 was passed on the reactor for 30 min to eliminate the influence of other gases in the air on the experimental results. Then, the reactor was placed vertically under a commercial 300-W Xe light source and a UV cut-off filter (420 nm) was used to remove UV light from the light source. Finally, the composition of the generated gas was analyzed by gas chromatography, and the hydrogen production was measured 7 times (30 min each time).

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production test: 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of solvent (deionized water: IPA = 9:1) and a 100 mL quartz vessel was taken into the photocatalytic reactor. Then, the reactor was placed vertically under a 300-W commercial Xe light source and O2 was continuously passed through the reactor during the reaction [20]. Finally, the liquid in the reactor was analyzed by UV spectrophotometer using potassium titanium oxalate as a titrant [21] for 7 extractions (30 min each).

The 0D/2D CQD/CNN heterojunction was synthesized using an electron-withdrawing carboxylated CQD-induced hydrothermal-calcination tandem synthesis strategy. Firstly, a series of carboxylated CQD were fabricated using 1,5-Diaminonaphthalene and acid reagents as precursors with equivalent mass via a surface charge state modulation strategy, and the resulting products were labeled as CQD-x (details are in the experimental section). Subsequently, these products were used as reactants and thermally polymerized with melamine under hydrothermal conditions. The reaction intermediates were calcined under an N2 environment at a certain temperature to obtain CNN-x samples. Photocatalytic measurements were conducted, and the most efficient sample was selected and labeled as CQD and CQD/CNN.

The CQD under investigation exhibit a uniform and well-dispersed black dot-like morphology with an average diameter of 3.57 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Notably, the TEM images of other CQD-x samples (Fig. S3) show that they possess comparable average sizes to the selected CQD, despite being synthesized using distinct acid reagents. Furthermore, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis of the selected CQD (Fig. 1b) revealed the presence of hexagonal lattice fringes with lattice plane distances of 0.20 nm [22]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to measure the height of CQD, as shown in Fig. 1c. The thickness of CQD was found to be around 1.0 nm, corresponding to two or three graphite layers. In contrast, pure CNN had a thickened sheet shape with a height of approximately 90 nm (Fig. S4). Upon introducing CQD and CQD-x into the CNN synthesis process, changes in the thickness of CNN were observable to the naked eye, as illustrated in Fig. 1d–f and S5 [23,24]. A typical AFM image and corresponding thickness analysis (Fig. 1f) provide evidence that the addition of CQD into the synthesis of CNN effectively reduces its thickness, with an average sample thickness of about 2.5 nm. In addition, the uniform distribution of CQD on the surface of CNN is observable, and the lattice spacing of the CQD (Fig. 1e) on the composite is consistent with that of the pure CQD in Fig. 1b, confirming the natural structure of CQD even after a series of reaction steps [25]. The ultra-thin nanosheet architectures endows the CQD/CNN largest surface area among these samples (Fig. S6 and Table S1).

Fig. 1g presents the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the pure CNN and CQD/CNN samples. The pure CNN sample exhibits two well-defined (100) and (002) planar peaks at 12.9° and 27.7°, representing in-plane packing and interfacial stacking of the heptazine units, respectively [26,27]. With the incorporation of CQD into the CNN matrix, the (100) peak of the CQD/CNN composite exhibited a blue shift to 12.50°, indicating an expansion of the crystal plane spacing. This expansion can be primarily attributed to the smaller size of CQD, facilitating its insertion into the layers of the CNN material and leading to the opening of the heptazine structure, thereby increasing the spacing between the catalyst layers. Moreover, a broad diffraction band in the range of 15° to 40° is observed for CQD/CNN, which suggests the modification of the general structure of g-C3N4 due to the presence of CQD. Similar XRD patterns are observed for the other CNN-x samples (Fig. S7) [28].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of all samples showed similar vibration bands on the surface (Fig. 1h and S8) [29]. The pure CNN and CQD/CNN samples show strong vibration bands at 775–855 and 1220–1460 cm−1, which are attributed to the tri-s-triazine rings and stretching vibrations of the C–N heterocycles, respectively. Nevertheless, a CO stretching vibration at around 1570–1700 cm−1 and a C–OH stretching vibration at ∼960–1180 and ∼3050–3300 cm−1 are observed in the CQD/CNN sample, which are attributed to the presence of carboxylated CQD. The FT-IR spectra of the other CNN-x samples (Fig. S8) were similar to those of pure CNN and CQD/CNN, confirming that the species of the surface functional groups of the produced samples are preserved after the addition of CQD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1i and S9) reveals the surface element chemical nature of the CNN and CQD/CNN. The oxygen content in the pure CNN and CQD/CNN samples is 2.04 and 1.89 at%, respectively (Table S2). The incorporation of CQD into the CNN matrix leads to a small change in the oxygen content in the samples. However, the atom percentage of CO and C–O functional groups in the CQD/CNN composite is higher than that in the pure CNN sample. Specifically, the atom ratio of CO and C–O in CQD/CNN increases from 1.68 to 2.70 (Table S3), indicating that carboxylated CQD play a crucial role in regulating the proportion of oxygen-containing surface functional groups of the samples. The deconvolution of C1s XPS spectra revealed the presence of three distinct peaks centered at 284.8, 287, and 288.9 eV, representing CC, C–O and sp2-hybridized carbon (N–CN) in the framework of CNN, respectively. Interestingly, a new shoulder peak at 288.2 eV was conspicuously observed in CQD/CNN composite, which was attributed to the prominent role of carboxyl groups in CQD (Fig. S9b) [30,31]. The N1s XPS spectra showed no significant changes, indicating that the nitrogen structure was well-preserved (Fig. S9c). This phenomenon was also evident in other samples, such as CNN-x (Figs. S10 and S11).

In order to examine the impact of CQD modification on the optical properties of CNN, we conducted an analysis of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the photocatalysts. Fig. 2 a demonstrates that CQD exhibits a pronounced absorbance in the wavelength range of 300–650 nm. Notably, upon incorporating CQD into the CNN matrix, the absorbance of the resulting CQD/CNN composite experiences a substantial enhancement in the visible region. The estimation of the band gap energy for the samples was carried out using the transformational Taus plots obtained from the Kubelka-Munk function, as shown in Fig. 2b. The calculated band gaps for CNN and CQD were found to be 2.74 and 2.86 eV, respectively [32,33]. Additionally, the VB-XPS results presented in Fig. 2c revealed a shift in the VB maxima for CNN and CQD to 1.92 and 2.52 V vs. NHE (NHE: normal hydrogen electrode), respectively. To further illustrate the impact of carboxylated CQD on the energy band structure, band structure diagrams were constructed for CNN and CQD, as depicted in Fig. 2d [34]. Notably, the CB of CQD was found to be lower by 0.48 eV compared to native CNN. This suggests that the incorporation of carboxylated CQD can effectively modulate the energy band structure of the composite material. The altered band structure holds potential implications for improved charge transfer and enhanced photocatalytic performance.

To gain a deeper understanding of the catalytic mechanism, the photophysical characteristics of the photocatalysts were investigated using PL spectra (Fig. 2e) and time-resolved transient PL decay spectra (Fig. 2f). The PL quenching of CQD/CNN heterostructure was significantly more pronounced than that of CNN, implying that the addition of CQD effectively suppressed the electron-hole recombination rate [35]. Furthermore, As depicted in Fig. 2f, the lifetime of the CQD/CNN photocatalyst material was determined to be 6.67 ns, which is significantly higher than that of the CNN material (4.49 ns). This is consistent with the previously reported phenomenon [26]. This result indicated that the addition of CQD enhanced the efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CQD/CNN [28]. These observations suggest that the addition of CQD enhances the photocatalytic activity of CQD/CNN by improving the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic reduction of water.

The influence of photoinduced carrier separation on photocatalytic process were further explored using transient photocurrent response technology. The measurements were performed on a fluorine tin oxide (FTO) electrode. As illustrated in Fig. S12, all samples exhibited superior photoactivity, as evidenced by the good response between the I-t curves of the samples upon intermittent visible-light illumination. Notably, the CQD/CNN presents the largest enhanced current response among all the samples, which suggests that this sample has more efficient charge separation of the h+ and e− pairs [30]. The electrochemical impedance (EIS) measurement was also performed to evaluate the charge transfer properties of the samples. As expected, the sample with smaller diameter indicates weaker impedance and more efficient charge transfer [36,37]. In Fig. S13, the CQD/CNN sample presents the smallest diameter among all the samples, indicating that the electron-withdrawing sites of carboxylated CQD can enhance the separation ability of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. Overall, these findings suggest that the photoinduced carrier separation is a critical factor that influences the photocatalytic performance of the samples. The carboxylated CQD in the CQD/CNN photocatalyst can act as electron acceptors, which promote the efficient separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on surface charge transfer of CQD/CNN
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the modulation of surface charge, we employed Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) to analyze the charge transport and recombination dynamics of the charge transporting layers [38]. The surface potential of the CQD/CNN composite was examined under both dark and 365 nm-UV light irradiation conditions, as presented in Fig. 3 . Under dark conditions, the acquired surface potential maps (Fig. 3b) revealed a slight potential difference (ΔE = 0.12 mV) for CQD/CNN, indicating a relatively low electron transfer rate. However, subsequent scanning at the same position after illumination resulted in an increased potential difference of 0.28 mV (Fig. 3c), indicative of the formation of a surface electric field and the promotion of carrier transfer [22,39–42]. These observations strongly suggest that carrier transfer on the surface of the sample is facilitated under illumination, leading to an enhancement in photocatalytic performance [43–45]. This finding is in agreement with the results obtained from the EIS test, which demonstrated effective charge transfer. Furthermore, it has been experimentally validated that electron-hole recombination is effectively inhibited in the PL test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of CQD/CNN for hydrogen production and hydrogen peroxide production
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic H2 production capacity of CNN and CQD/CNN was measured under visible light irradiation and the H2 product was ascertained by gas chromatography (Fig. 4 a and b). The CQD/CNN exhibited a markedly superior H2 production effect in comparison to pure CNN and CNN-x, as evidenced by Fig. S14. It can be seen intuitively from Fig. 4a that the H2 production of CQD/CNN increased with reaction times. Then, the H2 production rate of CQD/CNN under continuous 3.5 h photoluminescence illumination was gained to be 54.50 mmol g−1, whereas pure CNN only yielded 7.59 mmol g−1, representing a noteworthy seven-fold enhancement in performance over bulk CNN. The improved photocatalytic H2 production of CQD/CNN can be attributed to the ultra-thin nanosheet-like morphology, which provides a larger surface area for a greater number of reactive sites. Fig. S15 clearly demonstrates that the photocatalytic performance of the CQD/CNN catalyst utilized in our experiment surpasses the majority of catalysts in terms of hydrogen production efficiency [46–58]. Moreover, the cycle photocatalytic stability of CQD/CNN has also been carried out under continued visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 4b. It was observed that the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of CQD/CNN remained stable throughout four consecutive cycling photocatalytic experiments, highlighting the high stability of CQD/CNN, even after incorporating carboxylated CQD into the architecture of CNN [59–61]. The stability of the CQD/CNN catalyst after a cyclic stability test was thoroughly assessed using a combination of TEM, XRD, and FT-IR characterizations. TEM analysis, as depicted in Fig. S16a, showcases the uniform distribution of CQDs across the surface of the sample, indicating the catalyst's remarkable ability to maintain its morphology stability even after undergoing a rigorous 14-h cyclic testing period. Furthermore, the XRD and FT-IR analyses (Figs. S16b–c) reveal negligible alterations in the structure of the CQD/CNN catalyst following the cyclic stability test. The XRD patterns exhibit no significant changes in the diffraction peaks, indicating the preservation of the catalyst's structure. Similarly, the FT-IR spectra display no substantial shifts in the characteristic absorption bands, affirming the retention of the catalyst's chemical bonding configurations. These results collectively corroborate the exceptional stability of the CQD/CNN catalyst.

In addition to the H2 production application, the CQD/CNN photocatalyst was investigated for its potential application in generating value-added peroxide (H2O2) through oxygen reduction. The activity of H2O2 production was evaluated using IPA as the sacrificial agent under the visible light irradiation. The H2O2 concentration was found to increase with reaction times, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. The CQD/CNN photocatalyst achieved a maximum H2O2 concentration of 0.763 mmol/L, which is sixteen times higher than that of pure CNN (0.0475 mmol/L) [62]. The activity of O2 reduction to H2O2 was further assessed using a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) (Fig. S17), and the CQD/CNN photocatalyst was found to display lower onset potential than CNN, revealing a faster kinetics process. The H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer number (n) of CQD/CNN and CNN at different onset potentials were calculated using the ring disk current, and the results are presented in Fig. 4d. The CQD/CNN photocatalyst exhibited outstanding performance evaluated by RRDE, delivering H2O2 molar selectivity up to 82% and an excellent electron number located in 2.2 under the onset potential of 0.6 V [63]. These findings suggest that the CQD/CNN photocatalyst has great potential in generating value-added H2O2 through oxygen reduction under visible light irradiation.

Upon irradiation of visible light on the catalyst surface, a series of photochemical processes generate active intermediates such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and electrons (e−). To elucidate the contributions of these reactive species in the photocatalytic process, free radical capture experiments were conducted by adding scavengers to quench ·OH, ·O2 −, and e− during the photocatalytic process. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were utilized as scavengers for ·OH, ·O2 −, and e−, respectively. As evidenced by Fig. 4e, BQ showed a more pronounced inhibition of H2O2 production, suggesting that ·O2 − play a dominant role in the production of H2O2 [64,65].

CO2 and NH3 temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of CQD/CNN and CNN was also investigated to gain deeper insight into the Lewis acid and base active sites. The desorption curves were analyzed in detail, and the results were presented in Fig. 4f. The TPD experiments revealed that the physisorption of CO2 on CNN was comparatively stronger than on CQD/CNN at around 400 °C, confirming the presence of strong Lewis basic sites of CNN. Conversely, the strong peak around 350 °C of CQD/CNN in NH3-TPD demonstrated the chemisorption of NH3, implying the existence of strong acid sites of CQD/CNN attributed to the introduced carboxyl functional groups. Further, the O2-TPD data revealed that CQD/CNN exhibited a higher adsorption capacity of O2 than CNN, which supports the critical role of O2 adsorption in the overall process [66–68]. It can be inferred that the preferred adsorption of O2 of CQD/CNN can promote their two-electron reduction of O2 to H2O2, thereby showcasing their potential as an efficient catalyst. Overall, the TPD experiments offer valuable insights into the active sites of the materials and pave the way for the design and optimization of catalysts for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CO/C–O ratio on properties
ce:section.ce:para: Through elucidating the relationships between morphological/structural modulation and photocatalytic capability, it has been determined that the CO/C–O ratio exerts the most significant influence on performance. This finding suggests that the incorporation of carboxylated CQD can enhance the electron-withdrawing sites of CQD/CNN. The presence of electron-withdrawing groups enhances the electronegativity of the material, facilitates electron conduction, and promotes easier electron transfer. As a result, the performance of the heterojunctions is improved [13,69]. To test our hypothesis, we plotted the conduction band values (Fig. S18), PL intensity, and H2 production performance for all samples above were plotted against the atomic ratio of CO/C–O configurations based on O 1s XPS analysis, as depicted in Fig. 5 . Our analysis revealed a positive linear correlation between the atomic ratio of CO/C–O configurations and conduction band values and performance, while a negative linear relationship was observed with PL intensity. Specifically, the gradual increase of the CO/C–O ratio led to a positive correlation between the conduction band value and performance, as demonstrated in Fig. 5a–c. Conversely, the PL intensity decreased with the increase in the CO/C–O ratio. Moreover, we constructed a radar chart to determine the dominant influence of CO/C–O ratio, lifetime, and PL intensity on the performance of CQD/CNN. According to Fig. 5d, our analysis indicated that CO/C–O ratio had the greatest impact on the performance, indicating that the electron-withdrawing sites of CQD/CNN could be augmented by incorporating carboxylated CQD, thereby improving the H2 production performance of the composite. Additionally, the PL intensity was found to be the second most influential factor, suggesting that the suppression of the electron-hole recombination rate would enhance the H2 production performance. Overall, our findings provide crucial insights into the interplay between the CO/C–O ratio, electronic structure, and photocatalytic performance of CQD/CNN.

In summary, we devised a novel approach to fabricate high-performance photocatalytic materials by designing and synthesizing electron-withdrawing carboxylated CQD through surface charge state modulation. Subsequently, we developed 0D/2D CQD/CNN using a tandem synthesis strategy of CQD-induced hydrothermal-pyrolysis. The addition of CQD into the synthesis of CNN effectively reduces its thickness of CQD/CNN low to 2.5 nm, representing a noteworthy one thirty-sixth in height of bulk CNN. The carboxylated CQD play a crucial role in regulating the proportion of oxygen-containing surface functional groups of CQD/CNN. The CQD/CNN heterojunctions showed exceptional H2 production rates and demonstrated the ability to convert O2 into H2O2. The H2 production rate of CQD/CNN was gained to be 54.50 mmol g−1, whereas pure CNN only yielded 7.59 mmol g−1, representing a noteworthy seven-fold enhancement in performance over bulk CNN. Moreover, the CQD/CNN photocatalyst achieved a maximum H2O2 concentration of 0.763 mmol/L, which is sixteen times higher than that of pure CNN (0.0475 mmol/L). Through elucidating the relationships between morphological/structural modulation and photocatalytic capability, the CO/C–O ratio had the greatest impact on the performance, indicating that the electron-withdrawing sites of CQD/CNN could be augmented by incorporating carboxylated CQD, thereby improving the performance of the heterojunctions. These results have important implications for the design and optimization of CQD/CNN-based photocatalysts for efficient H2 and H2O2 production. In order to further expand the application of CQD/CNN, exploring its potential in CO2 reduction and organic matter degradation will be important directions for future exploration.
Data will be made available on request.

Li Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Zeming Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
